meta.title: Mother Ann Lee was Nuts: So, Why does the NPR Crowd Love Her?
meta.author: The Theology Pugcast

questions.question: What central tension do the hosts use to frame their discussion of the Shakers?
options: Strong doctrine but poor practical skill
Admired craftsmanship alongside deeply flawed theology
Political influence without any religious claims
Economic failure despite rapid demographic growth
questions.answer: Admired craftsmanship alongside deeply flawed theology

questions.question: According to the discussion, what is a practical long-term consequence of mandatory celibacy in a religious movement?
options: It guarantees doctrinal unity across generations
It forces reliance on conversion rather than family growth
It increases birth rates through communal adoption
It removes the need for institutional structures
questions.answer: It forces reliance on conversion rather than family growth

questions.question: Where did Ann Lee and a small group of followers settle after immigrating to America in 1774?
options: Near Albany, New York
Near Lexington, Kentucky
Near Hartford, Connecticut
Near Manchester, England
questions.answer: Near Albany, New York

questions.question: How do the hosts describe the relationship between early charismatic movements and ritual over time?
options: Charismatic movements stay permanently anti-ritual
Ritual appears only in movements with formal clergy
Spontaneous practices eventually become patterned traditions
Ritual emerges only after political state recognition
questions.answer: Spontaneous practices eventually become patterned traditions

questions.question: Which concept do the hosts identify as central to their theological critique of Shaker belief?
options: Predestinarian determinism
Overrealized eschatology
Radical sacramentalism
Liturgical minimalism
questions.answer: Overrealized eschatology

questions.question: In the conversation, what does 'overrealized eschatology' mainly refer to?
options: Delaying all hope to a distant future kingdom
Treating end-time realities as if fully present now
Rejecting all claims about Christ's return
Prioritizing church growth metrics over doctrine
questions.answer: Treating end-time realities as if fully present now

questions.question: What is 'Sunshine's first law of history' as quoted in the episode?
options: Never learn history from a movie
Only documentaries are reliable history
Primary sources always agree with each other
Biographies are more objective than archives
questions.answer: Never learn history from a movie

questions.question: Why do the hosts think modern progressive audiences often admire the Shakers?
options: Because Shakers pioneered modern electoral democracy
Because Shakers were doctrinally orthodox Protestants
Because selected Shaker traits align with current egalitarian and sustainability values
Because Shakers rejected all forms of craftsmanship
questions.answer: Because selected Shaker traits align with current egalitarian and sustainability values

questions.question: Which of the following is presented as a major Shaker legacy in the episode?
options: Expansion through missionary empires
A global seminary network
Influence on modern constitutional law
Durable, elegant craftsmanship and furniture
questions.answer: Durable, elegant craftsmanship and furniture

questions.question: How do the hosts describe Shaker governance in relation to men and women?
options: One centralized male-only ruling body
No governance structure at all
Parallel male and female oversight structures
Rotating rule by external clergy
questions.answer: Parallel male and female oversight structures

questions.question: Which architectural example is used to illustrate Shaker efficiency?
options: A fortress monastery with no windows
A glass basilica built over a river
A round barn with a central silo feeding radial stalls
A chapel designed for underground worship
questions.answer: A round barn with a central silo feeding radial stalls

questions.question: What broader historical principle do the hosts apply to religious movements like the Shakers?
options: Movements survive best by avoiding institutions
Any movement must institutionalize to pass on its legacy
Only state-backed churches can endure
Doctrinal diversity alone guarantees continuity
questions.answer: Any movement must institutionalize to pass on its legacy

questions.question: In the hosts' analysis, what danger follows when churches center growth on conversionism alone?
options: They become too sacramental and lose outreach
They eliminate all personal devotion
They risk demographic decline and eventual collapse
They stop producing any social institutions
questions.answer: They risk demographic decline and eventual collapse

questions.question: What comparison do the hosts draw between some modern evangelical practices and Shaker dynamics?
options: Both prioritize intergenerational catechesis above all
Both reject all forms of spontaneous prayer
Both emphasize household sacraments as the central marker
Both can privilege dramatic individual experience over inherited formation
questions.answer: Both can privilege dramatic individual experience over inherited formation

questions.question: How do the hosts characterize much historical filmmaking about religious figures?
options: As neutral presentation free from present-day agendas
As exact reconstruction based on complete archives
As shaped by current cultural and political priorities
As primarily theological rather than narrative
questions.answer: As shaped by current cultural and political priorities

questions.question: What does the episode suggest about American political rhetoric and religion?
options: Religion has become irrelevant to American public life
Only explicitly secular arguments succeed in U.S. politics
Major movements often seek religious legitimacy, even when hostile to orthodoxy
Religious language works only in local elections
questions.answer: Major movements often seek religious legitimacy, even when hostile to orthodoxy

questions.question: Which site is mentioned as the location of a surviving Shaker remnant community?
options: Sabbathday Lake, Maine
Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
Burlington, Vermont
Nashville, Tennessee
questions.answer: Sabbathday Lake, Maine

questions.question: How do the hosts interpret the irony of the Shaker legacy?
options: Their theology remained dominant, but their craft vanished
Their movement collapsed, but their aesthetic artifacts endure
Their institutions expanded, but their archives were lost
Their politics succeeded, but their theology was never developed
questions.answer: Their movement collapsed, but their aesthetic artifacts endure

questions.question: What function does the hosts' discussion of transhumanism serve?
options: It is presented as identical to classic sacramental theology
It is used as an example of a modernized, secularized eschatological impulse
It is introduced to explain Shaker farm tools
It is used to defend strict monastic celibacy
questions.answer: It is used as an example of a modernized, secularized eschatological impulse

questions.question: Which statement best captures the episode's warning for church practice today?
options: The best way to preserve faith is to avoid all inherited forms
Cultural relevance matters more than doctrinal coherence
Durable transmission of faith requires more than repeated outsider targeting
Institutional continuity is less important than aesthetic quality
questions.answer: Durable transmission of faith requires more than repeated outsider targeting

questions.question: What interpretation is offered for the disciplined beauty of Shaker craftsmanship?
options: It emerged from military engineering contracts
It arose from sublimating denied desires into highly ordered work
It was copied directly from Roman imperial styles
It came from abandoning all theological motives
questions.answer: It arose from sublimating denied desires into highly ordered work
